things.name: wavelet_peak_candidates snippet
things.description: Identifies potential peak candidates in scattering data using a continuous wavelet transform.
things.paper_title: SciPy Peak Finding Algorithms for SAXS/WAXS/GISAXS/GIWAXS Source docs: - SciPy signal peak finding reference:
things.code_snippet:
import numpy as np
from scipy.signal import find_peaks_cwt
def wavelet_peak_candidates(q, intensity, min_width=2, max_width=30):
    widths = np.arange(min_width, max_width + 1)
    peak_indices = find_peaks_cwt(intensity, widths)
    return [{"q": float(q[i]), "intensity": float(intensity[i])} for i in peak_indices]
things.code_description: Identifies potential peak candidates in scattering data using a continuous wavelet transform.

things.name: rank_peaks_by_prominence snippet
things.description: Ranks detected peaks by their prominence to handle sloping backgrounds and compare peak quality across different intensity scales.
things.paper_title: SciPy Peak Finding Algorithms for SAXS/WAXS/GISAXS/GIWAXS Source docs: - SciPy signal peak finding reference:
things.code_snippet:
import numpy as np
from scipy.signal import find_peaks, peak_prominences
def rank_peaks_by_prominence(q, intensity):
    y = np.asarray(intensity, dtype=float)
    peaks, _ = find_peaks(y, distance=8)
    prominences, left_bases, right_bases = peak_prominences(y, peaks)
    order = np.argsort(prominences)[::-1]
    return [
        {
            "q": float(q[peaks[i]]),
            "prominence": float(prominences[i]),
            "left_base_q": float(q[left_bases[i]]),
            "right_base_q": float(q[right_bases[i]]),
        }
        for i in order
    ]
things.code_description: Ranks detected peaks by their prominence to handle sloping backgrounds and compare peak quality across different intensity scales.

things.name: find_scattering_peaks snippet
things.description: Processes 1D scattering data by smoothing and baseline correction to identify peaks and calculate their positions, intensities, prominences, and widths.
things.paper_title: SciPy Peak Finding Algorithms for SAXS/WAXS/GISAXS/GIWAXS Source docs: - SciPy signal peak finding reference:
things.code_snippet:
import numpy as np
from scipy.signal import find_peaks, peak_widths, savgol_filter
def find_scattering_peaks(q, intensity):
    y = np.asarray(intensity, dtype=float)
    y = np.nan_to_num(y, nan=np.nanmedian(y))
    y_smooth = savgol_filter(y, window_length=11, polyorder=3)
    y_corrected = y_smooth - np.percentile(y_smooth, 10)
    peaks, props = find_peaks(
        y_corrected,
        prominence=0.05 * np.max(y_corrected),
        distance=8,
        width=2,
    )
    widths = peak_widths(y_corrected, peaks, rel_height=0.5)
    return [
        {
            "q": float(q[idx]),
            "intensity": float(y_corrected[idx]),
            "prominence": float(props["prominences"][i]),
            "fwhm_samples": float(widths[0][i]),
        }
        for i, idx in enumerate(peaks)
    ]
things.code_description: Processes 1D scattering data by smoothing and baseline correction to identify peaks and calculate their positions, intensities, prominences, and widths.

things.name: fit_gaussian_peaks snippet
things.description: Fits multiple Gaussian peaks and a linear background to 1D scattering data.
things.paper_title: # Peak Detection and Fitting Code Snippets Sources started from `resources.txt`: - SciPy: https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.signal.find_peaks.html - Astropy modeling:
things.code_snippet:
def fit_gaussian_peaks(x, y, peak_indices):
    model = LinearModel(prefix="bg_")
    params = model.make_params(slope=0.0, intercept=float(np.nanmedian(y)))
    for n, idx in enumerate(peak_indices):
        prefix = f"p{n}_"
        peak = GaussianModel(prefix=prefix)
        model += peak
        params.update(
            peak.make_params(
                center=float(x[idx]),
                amplitude=float(max(y[idx], 1e-12)),
                sigma=max(float(np.diff(x).mean() * 3), 1e-12),
            )
        )
        params[f"{prefix}sigma"].min = 0
    result = model.fit(y, params, x=x, nan_policy="omit")
    return result
things.code_description: Fits multiple Gaussian peaks and a linear background to 1D scattering data.

import pyFAI
def integrate_then_find_peaks(image, poni_file, *, npt=1000):
    ai = pyFAI.load(poni_file)
    result = ai.integrate1d(image, npt=npt, unit="q_A^-1")
    q = result.radial
    intensity = result.intensity
    peaks = detect_1d_peaks(q, intensity, prominence=0.03, min_distance=8)
    return q, intensity, peaks

things.name: detect_1d_peaks snippet
things.description: Detects peaks in 1D scattering curves (intensity vs q) using smoothing, baseline correction, and prominence filtering.
things.paper_title: # Peak Detection and Fitting Code Snippets Sources started from `resources.txt`: - SciPy: https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.signal.find_peaks.html - Astropy modeling:
things.code_snippet:
import numpy as np
from scipy.signal import find_peaks, peak_widths, savgol_filter
def detect_1d_peaks(q, intensity, *, prominence=0.05, min_distance=5):
    y = np.asarray(intensity, dtype=float)
    y = np.nan_to_num(y, nan=np.nanmedian(y))
    y_smooth = savgol_filter(y, window_length=11, polyorder=3)
    baseline = np.percentile(y_smooth, 10)
    y_corrected = y_smooth - baseline
    peaks, props = find_peaks(
        y_corrected,
        prominence=prominence * np.nanmax(y_corrected),
        distance=min_distance,
    )
    widths = peak_widths(y_corrected, peaks, rel_height=0.5)
    return [
        {
            "q": float(q[idx]),
            "intensity": float(y_corrected[idx]),
            "prominence": float(props["prominences"][i]),
            "fwhm_samples": float(widths[0][i]),
        }
        for i, idx in enumerate(peaks)
    ]
things.code_description: Detects peaks in 1D scattering curves (intensity vs q) using smoothing, baseline correction, and prominence filtering.

things.name: estimate_background_with_sigma_clipping snippet
things.description: Uses sigma clipping to estimate a smooth background and isolate excess intensity from crystalline peaks.
things.paper_title: pyFAI Peak-Finding and Scattering Reduction Algorithms Source docs used: - pyFAI stable docs: https://pyfai.readthedocs.io/en/stable/ - Calibration peak-picking: https://pyfai.readthedocs.io/en/stable/calibration.html
things.code_snippet:
import pyFAI
import numpy as np
def estimate_background_with_sigma_clipping(image, poni_file, mask=None, npt=2000):
    ai = pyFAI.load(poni_file)
    clipped = ai.sigma_clip_ng(
        image,
        npt=npt,
        unit="q_A^-1",
        mask=mask,
        thres=5.0,
        max_iter=5,
        error_model="poisson",
        method=("no", "csr", "cython"),
    )
    normal = ai.integrate1d(image, npt=npt, unit="q_A^-1", mask=mask)
    excess = normal.intensity - clipped.intensity
    return clipped.radial, clipped.intensity, excess
things.code_description: Uses sigma clipping to estimate a smooth background and isolate excess intensity from crystalline peaks.

things.code_snippet:
from pyFAI.massif import Massif
def pick_calibration_peaks_massif(image, seed_yx, mask=None, nmax=200):
    massif = Massif(image, mask=mask, median_prefilter=True)
    nearest = massif.nearest_peak(seed_yx)
    peaks = massif.find_peaks(nearest, nmax=nmax)
    return peaks
things.code_description: A wrapper function that uses the Massif class to find calibration peaks starting from a user-provided seed location.

things.name: monte_carlo_peak_seeds snippet
things.description: Generates random seed coordinates near an expected ring locus for peak picking.
things.paper_title: pyFAI Peak-Finding and Scattering Reduction Algorithms Source docs used: - pyFAI stable docs: https://pyfai.readthedocs.io/en/stable/ - Calibration peak-picking: https://pyfai.readthedocs.io/en/stable/calibration.html
things.code_snippet:
import random
def monte_carlo_peak_seeds(expected_ring_pixels, n_samples=200):
    seeds = []
    for _ in range(n_samples):
        y, x = random.choice(expected_ring_pixels)
        seeds.append((y + random.uniform(-3, 3), x + random.uniform(-3, 3)))
    return seeds
things.code_description: Generates random seed coordinates near an expected ring locus for peak picking.

things.paper_title: pyFAI Peak-Finding and Scattering Reduction Algorithms Source docs used: - pyFAI stable docs: https://pyfai.readthedocs.io/en/stable/ - Calibration peak-picking: https://pyfai.readthedocs.io/en/stable/calibration.html
things.code_snippet:
import numpy as np
from scipy.signal import find_peaks
from pyFAI.integrator.fiber import FiberIntegrator
def giwaxs_line_profile_peaks(image, poni_file, incident_angle_deg=0.12):
    fi = FiberIntegrator(poni_file)
    line = fi.integrate1d_grazing_incidence(
        data=image,
        sample_orientation=6,
        incident_angle=np.deg2rad(incident_angle_deg),
        npt_ip=1000,
        npt_oop=100,
        ip_range=(-7, 7),
        oop_range=(5.5, 6.25),
        vertical_integration=False,
        method=("no", "csr", "cython"),
    )
    x = line.integrated
    y = line.intensity
    peaks, props = find_peaks(y, prominence=0.03 * y.max(), distance=10)
    return [{"q_ip": float(x[p]), "intensity": float(y[p])} for p in peaks]
things.code_description: Custom wrapper that integrates GIWAXS data into a 1D line profile and identifies peaks using SciPy.
